I am a 65-year-old retired registered nurse.
I was a nurse for 40 years.
I absolutely think I was born to be a nurse as a little child growing up.
I always had a little injured animal of some type that I was nursing back to health.
It just was second nature to me.
And I loved being a nurse, not only for the challenge of it,
but you were meeting people who were at their worst point in life,
whether it was an accident,
not expected or a major surgery coming or a death in the family.
And you could be there and be their strength and somebody that they could lean
And it just,
it was an honor for me to be able to be there for people like that.
I really enjoyed my calling.
It was sad when I had to give it up.
I'm sure it was sad.
If you ask me,
I think you're right.
I think you were born to do that sort of thing.
You're so loving and caring and such a good person.
I'm not surprised to hear you say that.
Thank you.
You're welcome.
Joe, from what I understand, you think your mom might have had a dogman encounter of her own.
Why do you think that?
Well, we were talking one time about scary movies, and I think I was around maybe 12 or 13.
And she said, well, you know, we didn't have those when I was a kid growing up.
But I had something happen to me one time that I think would made anybody scared.
And I said, what was that?
And my mother was a tomboy.
She grew up in the hills of Arkansas.
She was a true hillbilly.
There was like 13 in the family.
And she could outrun most of her brothers.
And she loved basketball.
I mean, she was a sports girl.
And they had an outhouse that they had to use.
And it was set back away from their house.
And so she went out one night about one o'clock in the morning, had her little kerosene lantern,
and she went out there and they had a spade that they kept in it, not only to kind of
help hold the door closed, but sometimes snakes would be in there.
And so it was nothing to her just to take that and just hit it and cut its head off.
I mean, she was all tomboy.
And she went out there one night.
It was a full moon, so she was staying pretty good.
She took the lantern that had hardly any kerosene in it because she could see the trail.
And she got out there and was doing her business and something jumped and landed on the top of this outhouse.
And it startled her because she couldn't imagine why I could get up there so easy.
And it was walking around on top of it.
And, you know, she could tell it was big and heavy and it was sounding like it was on two feet.
And she was thinking, now that can't be.
I mean, we're up here.
It could be a mountain lion, you know, but I would think I would be hearing four.
and it walked back and forth on the top of that
and she could hear it kind of
like huffing a little bit and it jumped down and she could hear it coming around the outhouse on the outside and she was getting kind of spooked because most of the time animals don act like
that up there and it got over by the door and it kind of scratched on a little bit she was holding
that door shut and so she was pretty concerned and she thought well I'm not going to leave until
this thing gets tired and starts leaving itself and it kind of got quiet she thought well maybe
this is the time and then it landed back up on top of it again so she thought this thing was trying
to outweigh me so she thought i can play that game i'll just stay in here till morning if i have to
so she was sitting there pretty quiet and it's kind of getting quiet and then her lantern ran
out of the kerosene so now she's in the dark and she's got this spade and she's thinking you know
this is going to be my weapon so she hadn't heard anything for a while and so she opened up the door

found to lock that door. Now this thing is in the house and she can hear it coming up the stairs

I could kill you right now. I could have killed you out there. You know, don't ever hit me again.
She just like feels that's the thought being given to her. And it stayed on her for a while.
And then it slowly backed off and she heard it go down and go.
And then she heard the front door open and close.

especially when her dad woke up the next day and realized that whoever was out there didn't close it.

So she definitely wasn't an own up to it then.

Never saw anything like that again, but I certainly didn't try to go to the bathroom at night anymore either.
And so after I started learning about Dogman, I thought, you know, that's what that was.
It sounds like it.
Going from four feet to two feet?
Had to be.
I wish she was still alive so I could tell her.
That was a Dogman, Mom.
It wasn't a dream.
It was a Dogman.
It sure was.
Holy cow, what she must have gone through.
That is horrible.
That is awful.
You had your first dogman encounter one night when you were 17, Joe,
and it frightened you so much you jumped into bed with your parents.
Had you heard about cryptids before you had that experience?
And if you had, what were your thoughts on them?
I had not.
I had not.
We didn't even have a TV when I was young.
I mean, it was all work.
We worked for everything we had.
We had a little farm and it was not doing well and we were trying so hard
I had two older brothers and they were 11 and 13 when I was born
And they weren't expecting any more kids

They were kind of disappointed actually that they had another child because it going to be more expensive And then when my dad realized that I was a girl he was very upset He said he wish he had been a boy because they could work harder and help more
And here he is, he's got a daughter, what's he going to do with a daughter?
So it always made me feel kind of bad about that. So I quit going by Joyce when I was 10
And I started going by Joe because it didn't sound so girly.
And I tried my best to keep up with my brothers on everything, every kind of chore.
And one thing that my older brother always did, he tried to scare me.
And if I kind of yelled out a little bit or something, he would tease me and say, you sound like a girl.
So it was kind of an ongoing thing with him that you can't show fear and you definitely don't scream out.
So ironically, when I went and jumped in the middle of my mom and dad in bed afraid,
that was a big deal because I had never tried to show fear before.
So that lets you know how much it rocked my world.
I broke my own rule of showing no fear.
Well, you did, but it's totally understandable when you consider what happened to you.
That first encounter, that was awfully intense.
Not as intense as the later ones, but it definitely would get your motor running.
How long did it take after you had that first encounter, though, Joe,
before you found out that they're actually called dogmen?
Oh, it was decades.
I only found out, I'm going to say, four years ago.
I had no idea what to call it.
I always thought it was maybe some kind of a werewolf,
but then werewolves are supposed to be in the movies only,
or a demon from hell.
I wasn't sure what it was.
and then I actually stumbled onto some information about a dog man.
I had a friend that was listening to some podcasts

hey, wait a second, this sounds kind of familiar.

because you know so much about it, it's amazing.

that you can't even describe what it is or how it exists or why it exists?
You can't.
And I think that's why it traumatized me as much as it did for as long as it did.
Well, that's totally understandable.
Yeah, like you said, when you can hang a name on something that you're afraid of,
that does help.
It's not going to work wonders necessarily, but it does help, at least to a certain degree.
So I can understand why you say that.
All right, Joe, I know it's not going to be easy to talk about these experiences due to how traumatic they were,

Will do.
Well, as I told you, I grew up trying to be as tough as I could and not show fear,
just so that I had as much merit in my father's eyes as my two brothers.
And I really convinced myself that I didn't get scared.
And I decided at 17 that I wanted to become a nurse.
And like I said, I had always taken care of little animals,
and I just knew that's what I wanted to be.
So I went and applied, and they said, well, normally we don't take women in at 17,
but you seem to be a very mature.
17 and you will be 18 before you graduate. So we're willing to give you a try on this.
And I said, I will not let you down. And so I started my training. Well, it was harder on me
than I thought, because you can't be a kid. You know, when you're going in and seeing the things
that you're doing and learning how to do these things like on code teams and stuff, it's time
to be all adult. And so I had a couple of friends that I still had left over from school. I had
quit and got my GED so I could work on the farm because we were getting close to going under.
And I had a male friend that I was seeing on and off a little bit. And so we would get together
and go out and eat and just ride around and laugh. And I could kind of be a kid. And then I would
come in in the evenings, probably about 10, 30, 11 at night, go in, lay down, get some sleep.
And I would get up at three o'clock in the morning and start studying for a test I had that day.
So it was fresh in my mind and I would go in and take the test and I was doing a 4.0 average.
So I felt like that was working for me pretty good.
Well, I came in one night and the house that we had was way back in the middle of nothing.
Just woods and things all around you, very little acreage around it.

And there was a little stream that came off the Neosha River and it would flood easy So this house was set up on cinder blocks before the foundation because it had flooded into it before and it was a very
very old house. So I would come in at night. Of course, you're out in the country, there's no
street lights or anything to help you. It's pitch black. And my mother had a yellow bulb out on the
front porch that's supposed to let bugs come in and that was the only thing I had to try to get
into the house safely. Well, I was going up that one night and there was something that made this
unbelievable sound in the brush and the trees about 30 feet from the front door and I stopped
and I looked over and I thought, what in the world could be making a sound that big? And it sounded
bike, it was only on two feet. And so I literally walked over to the edge of that porch and I leaned
out that direction trying to see with the use of that little yellow bulb what was making that kind
of sound. And it got quiet. Whatever it was quit moving. And then I realized, well, everything's
quiet. There's nothing making any sound. And that was before I learned on programs like yours,
that that means there's probably something out there that you don't want to be outside with.
So I wasn't afraid at all. And in my little 17 year old brain, I decided that this was a buck
or something that had been trying to get through there and had kind of jumped up and maybe got his
horns hooked into some branches of this tree because it was quite a ways up on this tree.
that the branches were moving and he was jerking and trying to get, you know, unhooked and he was
up on his back feet. And that's how I could justify the sound of two feet and it being high in the
tree. So I literally clapped my hands and yelled, get out of here, just as loud and as gruff as I
could, trying to make it jump and maybe help it get loose. And, you know, there was a little bit
of a, oh, and I thought, wow, that didn't sound like anything like you would think a deer would do.
But I also told myself, well, at night, antelope seem bigger and better than they are,
because you can't see them. You only have sound. So it didn't scare me. I literally walked on in
the house, went to bed, and forgot about it. Two nights later, we were having a storm moving in,
and I always loved to lay in bed and watch the storms and watch the lightning.
The house that we were in, it had real tall, narrow windows,
and that's because it was built so far back that, you know,
where they had just kerosene lanterns to help them,
that they had the windows tall so no matter what time of day,
you could catch the sun and help light that room up better.
and I was in a bedroom that was the back wall of the house
and I had two windows with about a two and a half foot space in between them of wall
and one lit up the area that was my bed
and the other one was strategically put to where it would light up the hallway.
You know, so you didn't have a dark hall when you were going down in the early evenings.

and I was going to enjoy it.
I didn't have school the next day

what could be blocking the lightning coming.

There was something blocking it.

no, there's no way he could have something and be holding it
